does: This paper introduces ICED, a concept-level machine unlearning framework for Vision-Language Models that decomposes visual representations into sparse, non-negative combinations of semantic concepts derived from a task-specific vocabulary. Rather than operating at the image or instance level, the method uses a multimodal large language model to construct a compact concept vocabulary from the forgetting set, then performs targeted optimization to suppress specific concepts while preserving unrelated semantics within the same image and cross-modal knowledge globally. The approach provides explicit, interpretable control over which knowledge is removed through concept-level manipulation, enabling precise unlearning without collateral damage to related information.
connects: This work builds on the emerging problem of machine unlearning in foundation models, which has traditionally focused on instance-level or image-level removal approaches that struggle with entangled multi-concept images. It extends prior research on interpretability in neural networks, particularly work using concept-based explanations and sparse decompositions to understand model representations. The paper combines techniques from concept activation methods and sparse non-negative matrix factorization with advances in Vision-Language Models like CLIP, drawing on the recent surge of multimodal foundation model research. It also connects to the broader unlearning literature by addressing the challenge of selective forgetting while maintaining model utility, a core tension in prior work on machine unlearning and model editing.
enables: This framework opens avenues for more granular control over unlearning in large multimodal models, potentially enabling applications in privacy-preserving model deployment where specific concepts or sensitive associations must be removed without retraining. The interpretable concept decomposition approach could facilitate auditing and understanding what knowledge models retain, supporting efforts toward more transparent and controllable AI systems. Future work could extend this to other modalities or foundation models, develop better concept vocabulary construction methods, and explore how concept-level unlearning scales to larger, more complex concept spaces. The method also suggests pathways for fine-grained model editing and adaptation tasks beyond unlearning, where selective knowledge manipulation at the semantic level could improve model behavior in specialized domains.
